summary: Camille Fournier reflects on publishing Rent the Runway's engineering career ladder 10 years ago, one of the first public ladders with clear IC/management distinctions. She shares the open-source motivation behind publishing it and notes how the practice has become widespread across the industry. The post offers practical advice for organizations writing their own ladders, emphasizing the importance of details, honesty about career paths, and avoiding pure checklist approaches. She concludes by questioning whether ladders remain relevant in an era of 'flat organization' backlash, but argues that human nature and the need for transparent, equitable structures means this work still matters.
key_points: Publishing the RTR engineering ladder in 2015 helped spark an industry-wide movement toward transparent career frameworks
Career ladders should be more prescriptive than horoscopes - details and long-form explanations help flesh out expectations
Promotions should reflect organizational needs, not just individual skill checklists
Not every job family needs a ladder from bottom to top - be honest about realistic career paths
Despite 'flat organization' trends, human instincts toward hierarchy and the tyranny of structurelessness make career ladders still valuable
money_quotes: As an open source person, I believe in the value of sharing not only ideas but actual work for others to use, build upon, and expand, for the greater benefit of the tech community.

Most people want a starting point to play off of when designing organizational processes as much as they want one for technical work. Even if all the starting point inspires is 'ew, not this!', it gives you something to riff off of.
Your ladder should be more prescriptive than a horoscope.
Promotions are as much about the needs of the organization as the skills of the employees.
Don't promise people that they can become the VP of X just because some people do job X.
I'm not convinced that we'll overcome the human instinct to gather in groups and create leaders of those groups, and fight one another for power of those groups.
The Tyranny of Structurelessness has yet to be overcome.
key_insight: Career ladders remain essential infrastructure for equitable organizations because human nature inevitably creates hierarchies - the choice is whether those structures are transparent and intentional or hidden and arbitrary.
